---
title: "Preregistration: Megastudy testing 20 messaging interventions to strengthen trust in climate scientists in the US"
running-head: "Preregistration Megastudy Trust Climate Scientists"

author:
  - name: Matti Vuorre
    email: mjvuorre@uvt.nl
    orcid: 0000-0001-5052-066X
    url: https://www.tilburguniversity.edu/staff/m-j-vuorre
    corresponding: true
    equal-contributor: true
    # affiliation:
    #   - ref: 1

abstract: |
  Recently, a large-scale study across 55 countries found that trust in climate scientists is the strongest predictor of belief in climate change and support for climate policies. Strengthening trust in climate scientists may therefore be an important lever for accelerating climate action. However, there is currently little evidence on how to strengthen trust in climate scientists. We aim to address this gap by systematically identifying, testing, and comparing communication strategies designed to strengthen trust in climate scientists. To this end, we are conducting a megastudy—a large-scale field experiment in which many different interventions are tested simultaneously within a single, large sample using a common, objectively measured outcome. The study will be conducted in the United States, where climate research is particularly threatened by funding cuts and public beliefs about climate change are highly polarized. Through an open call, we invited researchers and practitioners to collaborate and received 107 intervention proposals. From these, we selected 20 interventions, ranging from reading about climate science to discussing the topic with LLMs.
bibliography: ../references.bib

# Introduction

Climate change is considered one of the most pressing societal issues (Rogelj et al., 2023), necessitating urgent action at both the policymaking and individual level (IPCC, 2023). To believe in human made climate change—and the fact that it is a problem—people need to trust climate scientists: we typically cannot observe for ouselves how human actions affect the climate—we have have to rely on the measures, models, and interpretations of climate scientists. A large-scale study across 55 countries found that trust in climate scientists was the strongest predictor of belief in climate change and support for climate policies [@todorovaMachineLearningIdentifies2025]. Strengthening trust in climate scientists may therefore be an important lever to accelerate climate action.

There is encouraging evidence on how to strengthen trust in scientists in general, but very few studies have targeted climate scientists in particular. For scientists in general, studies have found that using open-science practices (Rosman et al., 2022; J. Schneider et al., 2022; Song et al., 2022) and highlighting successful replications (Hendriks et al., 2020) can increase trust. One study found that presenting participants with an infographic about the scientific process slightly increased trust in scientists (Agley et al., 2021). Exercising intellectual humility—acknowledging the limits of one's knowledge— has been shown to increase trust in a fictive virologist and a fictive climate scientists [@koetkeEffectSeeingScientists2024]. In another study, participants tended to acribe more expertise—a dimensions of trustworthiness—to scientists, when they present two-sided rather than one-sided arguments (Hendriks et al., 2023). One study found that reading short biographies of scientists made participants perceive the scientists as more trustworthy, presumably demonstrating benevolence (Hautea et al., 2024). Studies targeting trust climate scientists in particular are rare. One study found that messages emphasizing the need for changes in individual behavior or in public policy to address climate change reduced trust in climate scientists [@palmDontTellMe2020]. Another study showed that participants perceived climate scientists as more skilled—a dimension of trustworthiness—after reading a text about the longstanding history and foundations of climate science [@orchinikLearningScientistsConsensus2024].

The lack of direct evidence on how to strengthen trust in climate scientists is an issue: While trust in scientists is moderately high globally [@colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025], climate scientists have consistently been found to be less trusted than scientists in general [@ghasemiGapsPublicTrust2025] and scientists from other disciplines [@druckmanContinuityChangeTrust2024; @schroderDontTellMe2023; @schugPublicPerceptionsTrustworthiness2024; @pfanderFrenchTrustMore2025; @gligoricHowSocialEvaluations2024]. It is therefore not clear how findings regarding scientists more generally transfer to climate scientists specifically.

Another issue is the comparability of current findings: It is difficult to compare the effectiveness of different communication strategies on trust in scientists, because of differences in sample selection, outcome measures, and experimental design. This is all the more important, as not all studies have produced positive results. For example, a registered report testing messages tailored to conservatives in the US did not change trust [@gligoricPoliticalIdeologyTrust2025]. Another example is uncertainty communication: One study found no significant effect of uncertainty communication in science museums (Altenmüller et al., 2024), and some studies even suggest that communicating uncertainty might reduce trust in scientists (C. R. Schneider et al., 2022; van der Bles et al., 2020).

Here, we aim to address these issues by systematically identifying, testing, and comparing different communication strategies to strengthen trust in climate scientists. To do so, we run a megastudy—"a massive field experiment in which many different treatments are tested synchronously in one large sample using a common, objectively measured outcome" [@milkmanMegastudiesImproveImpact2021]. Our megastudy comprises 20 text-based interventions issued from a crowdsourcing process. While trust in climate scientists is the main outcome, we will also assess the interventions' impact on various other outcome variables, including, for instance, belief in climate change, support for climate policies and public research funding, and taking individual action to foster climate research (by making a donation a research institution). The results of our study therefore have the potential to inform the communication strategies of a wide range of actors—e.g., universities, research institutes, governments, or NGOs—looking to rally support in the fight against climate change.

# Research Questions

Our primary research question is: Which messaging interventions significantly increase trust in climate scientists? There are two groups of secondary research questions: First, how do the messaging interventions affect other outcomes of interests? Second, which variables moderate the effects of the messaging interventions?

# Methods

## Ethics

::: callout-caution
tbd
:::

## Messaging interventions

We used an open call for interventions designed to strengthen trust in climate scientists. We received 105 intervention proposals[^1] from 80 different research teams[^2] (25 teams submitted two interventions), from 65 different institutions.

[^1]: We originally received 107 proposals, but two got retracted from the authoring teams shortly after the submission deadline.

[^2]: some "teams" are actually individual researchers---24 of the 105 submissions are single-authored

An expert reviewer team selected 20 promising treatments for experimental testing. The expert reviewer team (@tbl-reviewers) consisted of three members of the research team (Madalina Vlasceanu, Sara Constantino, Viktoria Cologna) and an advisory board, consisting of 12 researchers and one practitioner. The selection process was fully anonymized---only the research lead (Jan Pfänder) could link submissions to their authors, and did not participate in the rating process. For details on the selection process, see the appendix (@sec-selection)

## Procedure and Design

### Main experiment

The main experiment will have a 22 conditions (20 interventions, 1 main control group, 1 interactive control group), between-subjects design.

The study consists of three parts: (i) pre-treatment, (ii) treatment, (iii) post-treatment. In the first part of the main experiment, participants will complete a short demographic questionnaire, including questions on their age, gender, education, and postcode. Participants will also respond to several additional variables, which we intend to test as moderator variables. These variables include XX. In the second part of the main experiment, participants will be randomly assigned to either one of the 22 conditions. In each of the 20 intervention conditions, participants will be asked to read the corresponding materials. In the main control condition, participants will be randomly assigned to read one of three neutral texts that are unrelated to climate change. The texts are about (a) the history of neckties, (b) the rules of baseball, and (c) different types of dances. Using several different control conditions minimizes the chance of unintended effects of any particular text on the outcome variables. In the interactive control condition, participants are prompted to have a conversation with an LLM on one of the same three unrelated topics from the main control group. Participants will asked to choose one of the topics. The LLM will then initiate a conversation. In the third part of the main experiment, we will assess outcome variables. Our main outcome of interest is trust in climate scientists. We will also measure secondary outcome variables, including XX.

\[table trust\]

\[table demographics & other pre-treatment variables\]

\[table secondary outcome variables\]

### Follow-up survey

The follow-up survey tests the persistence of the most successful interventions \~1 week after the main experiment. For the follow-up survey, we will reassess the samples from the 5 most successful interventions, as well as the main control group, on the outcome measures. If the one of the 5 most successful interventions is an LLM-based intervention, we will also reassess the interactive control group.

\[Add the bit on hidden purpose design\]

### Outcomes

### Main outcome

The main outcome is a multidimensional measure of trust in climate scientists (@tbl-trust). To make sure that all interventions would focus on targeting this outcome, we communicated no secondary outcomes during the call for intervention submissions (see next below).

::: {.cell-output-display}

## Sampling Plan


::: {.cell}

:::



::: {.cell}

:::


Participants will be recruited from a national, non-probability panel of American residents provided by CloudResearch. The sample will be quota-matched to the U.S. population for several demographic variables, including gender, age, race/ethnicity, education, and region. CloudResearch has been found to offer higher quality data than other survey providers, in particular MTurk [@kayWhyYouShouldnt2025]. We will only recruit adult participants who are more than 18 years old.

Data collection will be stopped as soon as we have collected complete responses from 22,000 participants (1,000 participants per experimental condition). Before treatment assignment, we will deploy a series of attention and bot detection checks. Participants will be informed right after the tests on whether they passed or not. Only those participants who have passed will be able to continue and complete the study.

According to Monte Carlo power simulations, a sample size of N = 22,000 (1,000 participants per experimental condition) would allow us to detect our smallest effect of interest, Cohen's d = 0.2, with statistical power of nearly 100% (@fig-power). An effect size of Cohen's d = 0.2 is typically considered small in behavioral sciences. In a pilot sample (N = 76), this minimal effect size of interest would translate to a 3-point change of the sample mean on a scale from 0, very low trust, to 100, very high trust (see @fig-pilot). In the simulations, we adjusted p-values for multiple testing via the Benjamini–Hochberg false discovery rate (FDR) procedure, and used $\alpha$ = .05 as the cutoff point for statistical significance. More information on the power simulations can be found in the Appendix (@sec-power).


::: {.cell layout-ncol="2"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Distribution of trust from the control condition (N=XX) of a pilot study of one of the submitted interventions.](preregistration_files/figure-docx/fig-pilot-1.png){#fig-pilot-1}
:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Translation of standardizes effect sizes tested in the power simulation to the original 0-100 trust scale.](preregistration_files/figure-docx/fig-pilot-2.png){#fig-pilot-2}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Results of the power simulation. Power is defined as the average share of statistically significant effects within a study, across 1,000 simulated studies. The plot shows power as a function of sample size (n per experimental condition, with equal sample size assumed for all 21 conditions). The two curves represent non-adjusted vs. adjusted p-values. The three facets represent different effect sizes.](preregistration_files/figure-docx/fig-power-1.png){#fig-power}
:::
:::


## Analysis plan

### Main treatment effects

We will test the effects of each of the treatments relative to the control condition with ordinary least squares regression. We will use heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors to ensure valid statistical inference in the presence of potentially unequal error variances across experimental conditions.

For all outcome variables, we will separately regress our post-treatment outcome variables on a categorical variable for the experimental condition, using the main control condition as the baseline category. For these analyses, we will exclude the interactive control condition. In the regression model, the categorical variable for experimental condition will be translated into a series of dummy variables, one for each intervention, with the control condition as the reference category in each case. 

Formally, for each outcome $Y_i$ (e.g., trust in scientists), we estimate the following model:

$$
Y_i = \beta_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_k \, \mathbb{1}(\text{Condition}_i = k) + \mathbf{X}_i \boldsymbol{\gamma} + \varepsilon_i,
$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\text{Condition}_i = k)$ is a dummy variable indicating assignment to intervention $k$, with the main control condition serving as the omitted reference category. The interactive control condition is excluded from the estimation sample. $\mathbf{X}_i$ denotes a (possibly empty) vector of pre-specified covariates, and $\varepsilon_i$ is an error term. All models are estimated using ordinary least squares with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors.

For each outcome, we test the null hypothesis $H_0: \beta_k = 0$ for each intervention $k$, corresponding to no difference relative to the control condition. To account for multiple comparisons across the 20 interventions within a given outcome, we adjust p-values using the Benjamini–Hochberg (or false discovery rate, FDR) procedure. We will not apply correction for muliple hypothesis testing across the different outcomes.


::: {.cell}

:::




::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

 [4] "intervention_10"     "intervention_11"     "intervention_12"    
 [7] "intervention_13"     "intervention_14"     "intervention_15"    
[10] "intervention_16"     "intervention_17"     "intervention_18"    
[13] "intervention_19"     "intervention_2"      "intervention_20"    
[16] "intervention_3"      "intervention_4"      "intervention_5"
```

:::

::: {.cell}

![](preregistration_files/figure-docx/fig-main-models-1.png){#fig-main-models}
:::
:::


### Exclusions

First, we do not allow for any individual participant taking part in our study several times. In cases with a duplicated participant ID, we will only keep the first case. Second, we will exclude participants who failed a series of attention and bot detection checks. These checks will be run before treatment assignment to avoid post-treatment bias [@montgomeryHowConditioningPosttreatment2018].

### Attrition and missing values

Since we do not force response on all variables in our survey, it is possible that we have missing values, even from participants who complete the survey. Our rule for handling missing values is as follows: A participant who has missing values for one or multiple primary dependent variables will still be included in the analyses on the primary dependent variable(s) for which they provided data.

It is further possible that we have attrition in our data, as even participants who pass the attention checks drop out of the survey after treatment assignment, and before completion. In line with our rule for missing values, we will consider these participants for all analysis regarding variables they provided data on. 

To assess whether attrition is systematically related to experimental condition, we will conduct outcome-specific attrition tests. For each outcome, we will estimate an ordinary least squares regression in which an indicator for survey completion (dependent variable) is regressed on the experimental condition (independent variable). We will use a heteroskedasticity-robust F-test to assess the joint significance (testing whether all treatment effects are zero at the same time) of the treatment indicators.

If there is evidence of differential attrition for a given outcome, we will conduct a robustness check by re-estimating the preregistered model for that outcome using inverse-probability weighting based on predicted survey completion.


::: {.cell}

:::



If we find evidence of differential attrition, we will conduct a robustness check by rerunning our preregistered models using inverse-probability weighting (IPW). IPW is a procedure of adjusting for missing data, by give greater weight to participants who had were more similar to participants who attrited. Specifically, for each participant, we estimate the probability of giving a valid answer, using random forests, with the experimental condition and several demographic variables as predictors. We then calculate a weight for each participant, taking the inverse of the predicted probabilities of providing a valid answer. As a consequence, participants who were more less likely to give an answer (i.e. more likely to attrite) receive a greater weight. These weights are incorporated into our regression models to obtain estimates that are robust to differential attrition.


::: {.cell}

:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](preregistration_files/figure-docx/fig-comparison-ipw-vs-unweighted-1.png){#fig-comparison-ipw-vs-unweighted}
:::
:::


### Moderator analyses

::: callout-caution
tbd
:::

We will calculate a separate regression for each moderator, by adding the moderator variable as a predictor variable to the ordinary least squares regressions on the main treatment effects.

# Appendix

## Interventions {#sec-interventions

\[Make a table with all interventions and print it here\]

### Selection of interventions {#sec-selection}

The selection process consisted of two steps: first, independent reviews, second, plenary discussion and final selection.

#### Independent reviews

Each of the 105 submission was independently reviewed by three randomly assigned reviewers[^3]. Reviewers provided ratings on four dimensions:

[^3]: 10 reviewers rated 24 interventions, 3 reviewers rated 25 interventions

**1. Theoretical grounding** Is the intervention based on sound and convincing theory?

**2. Theoretical insight** Would testing this intervention advance theoretical understanding? (Interventions with clear, single mechanisms may offer stronger insight than those combining many mechanisms.)

**3. Odds of success** How plausible is it that the intervention will work in our study? Helpful questions include: - Has it been tested before? - Was the context comparable (U.S. sample, setting, outcomes)? - How large were the effect sizes?

**4. Practical relevance** How relevant and scalable is the intervention in the real world? (E.g., can it be implemented easily? Is it feasible at scale?)

All ratings were given on a 0–10 scale (0 = “Not at all / Very weak”; 10 = “Completely / Very strong”). In addition to the numeric ratings, reviewers were encouraged to leave comments to put their ratings into context, and raise points worthwhile discussing during the plenary session.

Based on these independent reviews, we ranked the interventions. For this ranking, we first calculated reviewer specific weighted scores. Each reviewer assigned a weight to each of the four evaluation criteria. As shown in @fig-weights, the subjective weights differed considerably between reviewers, but odds of success was considered most important, and theoretical insight least important, on average.


::: {.cell}

:::



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Distribtions of weights assigned by reviewers. Each dot corresponds to one reviewer's weight for the respective rating dimension.](preregistration_files/figure-docx/fig-weights-1.png){#fig-weights}
:::
:::


For each reviewer, we computed a weighted average score for each intervention they reviewed. based on their own specific weights. Let $w_{r,c}$ be the weight reviewer $r$ gives to criterion $c$, and $x_{r,i,c}$ be their rating for intervention i on that criterion. Then the review score is:

$$
\text{score}_{r,i} = \frac{\sum_{c \in \text{rating\_vars}} w_{r,c} \cdot x_{r,i,c}}{\sum_{c \in \text{rating\_vars}} w_{r,c}} 
$$

Since the weights all add up to 1, this simplifies to:

$$
\text{score}_{r,i} = \sum_{c \in \text{rating\_vars}} w_{r,c} \cdot x_{r,i,c}.
$$

We then standardized the reviewers' scores. Reviewers tend to use the rating scales differently: some use the whole scale, some use a narrow band; some generally assign higher scores, some generally lower scores. We therefore standardize the reviewer's scores by z-scoring them. Let: $\text{score}_{r,i}$ be the weighted score reviewer $r$ assigned to intervention $i$, $\mu_r$ be the mean of reviewer $r$'s scores across all interventions they rated, $\sigma_r$ be the standard deviation of reviewer $r$'s scores. Then the standardized score is:

$$
\text{zscore}_{r,i}
= \frac{\text{score}_{r,i} - \mu_r}{\sigma_r}.
$$

This normalization placed all reviews on a common scale in units of (reviewer specific) standard deviation (with mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$).

#### Plenary meeting and final selection

We used the standardized aggregate scores from the independent reviews as the baseline for our final selection: by default, the top 20 ranking interventions were selected for testing. However, we held a plenary meeting with the entire research team and advisory board to discuss qualitative reviewer comments and make a final selection. During this meeting we agreed to remove interventions that were not not ready to implement and interventions that used fictional characters. To avoid redundancy, we further agreed on merging similar interventions that offered complementary elements. In this case, all teams involved in the merge were contacted to submit a revised intervention together, and all were offered co-authorship. When similar interventions did not show potential to complement each other, we selected the one with the highest rank and removed the others. We also removed two interventions on which major concerns regarding their effectiveness were raised during the discussion, and in qualitative reviewer comments. As a rule, interventions that were removed were replaced by the next-highest ranked intervention. There was only one exception to this rule: an LLM-based discussion intervention. We decided to include this intervention because we had very few dynamic interventions in our selection, and we agreed that the intervention was one of the most promising LLM-based interventions.

#### Reviews

We had two rounds of reviews. The first round was based on feedback from the individual reviews and the plenary session. Authors were given one week to submit revised versions of their stimuli. Each member of the expert reviewer team then got a chance to review all interventions again, and provide additional comments. The research team edited these comments into final revision requests. Authors were, again, given one week to address these final revision requests.

\[be more specific here on how many interventions needed revisions at each step\]

## Control conditions {#sec-controls}

### Main Control

#### The History of Neckties

Neckties date back hundreds of years, coming into existence as the direct result of a war. In 1660, in celebration of its hard-fought victory over the Ottoman Empire, a regiment from Croatia (then part of the Habsburg Monarchy) visited Paris. The soldiers were presented as heroes to Louis XIV, a monarch well known for his tendency toward personal adornment. The officers of this regiment were wearing brightly colored handkerchiefs fashioned of silk around their necks. These neck cloths - which probably descended from the Roman fascalia worn by orators to warm the vocal chords - struck the fancy of the king, and he soon made them an insignia of royalty as he created a regiment of Royal Cravattes. Vanity reigns supreme. The word "cravat," is derived from the word "Croat." It wasn't long before this new style crossed the channel to England. Soon, no gentleman would have considered himself well-dressed without sporting some sort of cloth around his neck-the more decorative, the better. At times, cravats were worn so high that a man could not move his head without turning his whole body. There were even reports of cravats worn so thick that they stopped sword thrusts. The various styles knew no bounds, as cravats of tasseled strings, plaid scarves, tufts and bows of ribbon, lace and embroidered linen all had their staunch adherents. How can we account for the continued popularity of neckties? For years, fashion historians and sociologists predicted their demise - the one element of a man's attire with no obvious function. Perhaps they are merely part of an inherited tradition. As long as world leaders continue to wear ties, the youth of the world will follow suit and ties will remain a key component to any man's professional wardrobe. Moreover, the adaptability of neckties to various styles and occasions ensures their enduring relevance in the ever-evolving landscape of men's fashion.

### The Rules of Baseball

Baseball, a quintessential American pastime, is a sport rooted in tradition and governed by a set of rules that define its unique charm. Understanding these rules is essential to fully appreciate the game. At its core, baseball consists of two teams, each aiming to score more runs than their opponent. The game unfolds over nine innings, with each team taking turns at bat and in the field. The offensive team strives to hit the ball thrown by the pitcher and advance around a series of bases, while the defensive team aims Page 26 of 72 to record outs by catching the ball or tagging runners. Key rules in baseball include the threestrike rule, which requires a batter to either hit the ball or accumulate three strikes to be called out, and the four-ball rule, granting a batter a free walk to first base if the pitcher throws four balls outside the strike zone. Additionally, there are regulations governing fair and foul balls, baserunning, fielding, and pitching techniques. Baseball's rules ensure fairness, strategy, and thrilling moments, making it a captivating and beloved sport cherished by fans worldwide. Moreover, baseball's rich history and cultural significance have led to the development of various traditions and rituals that further enhance the game-day experience. From the ceremonial first pitch to the seventh-inning stretch, fans eagerly participate in these time-honored customs, adding to the atmosphere of camaraderie and excitement in stadiums across the country. These traditions not only connect fans to the sport's past but also contribute to the sense of community and shared identity among baseball enthusiasts. The ups and downs of a baseball game mirror life’s challenges and triumphs, reminding us to stay resilient in the face of adversity and to celebrate our successes with humility. Through its timeless appeal, baseball continues to inspire generations, fostering a sense of unity and shared passion that transcends boundaries.

### Different Types of Dances

Dance, a universal form of expression, encompasses a diverse array of styles and traditions, each brimming with unique characteristics and cultural significance. From the refined movements of classical ballet to the vivacious rhythms of Latin dances and the dynamic energy of hip-hop, the world of dance offers a rich tapestry of experiences for enthusiasts of all backgrounds. Classical ballet, with its graceful movements and precise technique, epitomizes elegance and storytelling, with iconic productions like Swan Lake and The Nutcracker captivating audiences worldwide. On the other hand, contemporary dance thrives on innovation and fluidity, blending elements from various styles to create cuttingedge choreography that challenges conventional boundaries. Latin dances such as salsa, tango, and samba exude the passionate spirit and infectious joy of Latin American cultures, fostering connections and celebrations through movement and music. Meanwhile, hip-hop, rooted in African-American and urban communities, pulsates with its high-energy performances and expressive gestures, encompassing a wide range of styles from breaking to popping and locking. Beyond these well-known genres, ballroom, jazz, tap, and folk dances each boast Page 27 of 72 their own rich heritage and artistic nuances, while contemporary cultural dances continue to evolve and reflect the ever-changing global landscape. In essence, the diverse world of dance serves as a canvas for human expression, allowing dancers to convey emotions, tell stories, and forge connections with audiences in deeply resonant ways. Through its universal language, dance transcends cultural barriers, uniting people of different backgrounds and fostering understanding and appreciation for our shared humanity. It stands as a testament to the enduring power of creativity and connection in our world. Dance serves as a bridge between generations, preserving heritage while also embracing innovation and evolution. Moreover, it offers individuals a form of self-expression and liberation, allowing them to communicate emotions and experiences beyond words. As such, dance has the potential to inspire, uplift, and transform both individuals and communities.

## Power simulations {#sec-power}

To calculate statistical power, we set our smallest effect of interest for an intervention to Cohen's d = 0.2. This effect is typically considered small in behavioral sciences. Monte Carlo simulations suggested that, with 1,000 participants per experimental condition, we would achieve statistical power of nearly 100% \[GIVE EXACT NUMBER\] (@fig-power).

For more realistic simulations, we used pilot data from one of the selected interventions. We used the control condition from this pilot data (N = 76). @fig-pilot illustrates the distribution of trust in climate scientists in this sample (left), and how different hypothetical standardized effect sizes would shift the sample mean.

In our simulations, we estimated statistical power for different combinations of sample- and effect size parameters. For each of these combinations, we ran 1,000 simulations. Effect sizes were standardized (Cohen's d) and sample sizes are reported per experimental arm (i.e., a sample size of 1,000, in a design with 21 arms, corresponds to a total sample size of 21,000)[^4]

[^4]: Note that we simulated our main design, with only trust as the main outcome variable, and only the main control condition, not the additional LLM control condition.

Each simulation run generated data set, which reflected the planned megastudy design with 20 interventions and a shared control group. For more realistic distributions of our outcome variable trust in climate scientists, scores were generated by re-sampling with replacement from the empirical distribution observed in pilot data. Treatment effects were simulated by adding a standardized effect size (Cohen's d) to the intervention conditions, but not to the control condition. All interventions were assumed to have the same effect.

We translated the standardized effect sizes into point differences on the original 0 to 100 trust scale, by multiplying the standardized effect with the standard deviation of the simulated sample. Because standard deviations could vary slightly between simulations, the translated effect sizes could, too.\[These changes are minimal in high-powered samples. The expected value of the standard deviations is the standard deviation of the pilot data, sd = 14.83\]. Outcomes were constrained to the original 0–100 scale. This way, our simulation realistically mimicked potential floor/ceiling effects. Each simulated dataset was analyzed using a linear regression model predicting the outcome from each experimental condition relative to the control group, yielding 20 different treatment effect estimates.

Statistical significance was evaluated at $\alpha$ = .05 using both uncorrected p-values and p-values adjusted for multiple testing via the Benjamini–Hochberg false discovery rate (FDR) procedure. We defined statistical power as the proportion of intervention effects reaching significance within a single simulated data set. Our final power estimate for any combination of sample- and effect sizes was the average within-sample power across the 1,000 simulations.
